title: Meet Your Brain
brainConcept: The teen brain is highly plastic: the prefrontal cortex is still developing while the emotional/reward system is very active.
objective: I can explain why my brain is especially changeable right now and how that affects my reactions.

flow.connect: Welcome to Brain Lab. Quick round: each person names a skill they leveled up through practice (a sport, game, instrument) and roughly how long it took.
flow.teach: Your brain is rewiring faster now than almost any other time in life. The reward/emotion system (amygdala + dopamine) is in high gear, while the prefrontal cortex (the planner/brake) is still wiring up into your mid-20s. That's why big reactions can outrun good judgment — and why practicing these skills now sticks especially well.
flow.practice: Quick retrieval challenge with a new fact, then recall it 60 seconds later — proving practice + recall builds memory. Discuss: where could 'practice rewires me' apply in your life?
flow.close: Each person rates how 'in development' their brake feels in the moment (1-5) and gives an example.

contentQuestions.text: Why are big emotional reactions common for your age, brain-wise?
What does it mean that your prefrontal cortex is still developing?
How could knowing your brain is highly plastic change how you approach a hard skill?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what two brain systems did we contrast today?
Where this week did your 'gas pedal' move faster than your 'brake'?
What skill could you rewire if you practiced it consistently?

title: Spot Your Signals
brainConcept: Attention is a limited resource; emotions produce predictable physical and mental signals you can learn to detect early.
objective: I can identify my personal early-warning signals for strong emotions.

flow.connect: Echo: one sentence on why the teen brain reacts strongly. Then a 20-second attention test (track one sound; notice when the spotlight slips).
flow.teach: Self-control starts with self-detection. Emotions show up as body signals (tense shoulders, hot face, shallow breath) and mental signals (racing thoughts, tunnel vision). Catching them at a 3/10 is far easier than at an 8/10.
flow.practice: Personal signal map: each person lists their top two body signals and one thought-signal for anger and for anxiety, plus the intensity where they usually notice it now.
flow.close: Each person shares one signal they want to start catching earlier.

contentQuestions.text: What are your earliest body and thought signals for a strong emotion?
Why is it easier to manage a feeling at a 3 than at an 8?
How does a strong emotion shrink your attention spotlight?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what's the difference between a body signal and a thought signal?
When did you catch a signal early this week?
Which emotion sneaks up on you the most?

title: Catch the Wave
brainConcept: Labeling an emotion reduces amygdala activity and re-engages the prefrontal cortex (affect labeling).
objective: I can interrupt an emotional spike with a labeling or regulation strategy.

flow.connect: Echo: each person names one early-warning signal. Then a single round of paced breathing to set the tone.
flow.teach: When the amygdala spikes, the thinking brain goes partly offline ('flipping your lid'). Brain-imaging research shows that putting a feeling into words lowers amygdala activity and brings the prefrontal cortex back. Pair that with a physiological reset (long exhale) and you regain control.
flow.practice: Teach the physiological sigh (double inhale, long exhale) for two rounds. Then rehearse the combo: silently label ('I'm frustrated') + long exhale. Pair-share a recent spike and which tool would've helped.
flow.close: Each person commits to one tool and a realistic trigger situation to use it in.

contentQuestions.text: What happens in the brain when you 'flip your lid'?
Why does naming an emotion lower its intensity?
Which reset works best for you, and when?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what does affect labeling do to the amygdala?
Describe a spike you caught this week and what you did.
What trigger should you pre-plan a tool for?

title: Aim Your Spotlight
brainConcept: Multitasking is task-switching with a cost; single-focus and managing triggers protect working memory.
objective: I can set up my focus and cut distractions during a task.
materials: None (optional: phones face-down as a live demo).

flow.connect: Echo: each person names a regulation tool. Then a 30-second focus sprint with a deliberate distraction (a noise) to feel the pull.
flow.teach: There's no real multitasking — your brain switches and pays a 'tax' each time, weakening memory. Phones are engineered to grab the spotlight (dopamine hits). Focus is a setup problem: remove triggers, single-task, and re-aim fast when you drift.
flow.practice: Each person designs a 'focus setup' for homework tonight: where the phone goes, one task at a time, and a re-aim cue. Share and get one suggestion from a partner.
flow.close: Each person states their phone plan for tonight's focus block.

contentQuestions.text: Why is multitasking actually costly for your brain?
How are apps designed to capture your spotlight?
What's one change that would most improve your focus?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what is the hidden cost of task-switching?
How did your focus setup work this week?
What distraction is hardest for you to control?

title: Read the Room
brainConcept: The social brain infers others' states from limited cues — and often fills gaps with assumptions.
objective: I can read social cues and check my assumptions before reacting.

flow.connect: Echo: each person shares how their focus setup went. Then a quick 'read the tone' clip-free demo (leader says 'fine' three ways).
flow.teach: Your brain reads tone, face, and body to infer feelings — but it also guesses, and guesses can be wrong (especially over text, where tone is missing). Reading the room means noticing cues AND checking your story before reacting.
flow.practice: Ambiguous scenario: a friend leaves you on read. Brainstorm 3 possible explanations (not just the worst one). Discuss how a check-in beats an assumption.
flow.close: Each person names one assumption they'll check instead of believing automatically.

contentQuestions.text: Why is it easy to misread tone over text?
What's the difference between observing a cue and assuming a meaning?
How can checking an assumption prevent conflict?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: name two social cues your brain reads.
When did you check an assumption this week instead of reacting?
Which situations make you jump to the worst explanation?

title: Walk in Their Shoes
brainConcept: Perspective-taking builds a mental model of another's viewpoint by linking to your own experiences (Velcro).
objective: I can take another person's perspective even when I disagree with them.

flow.connect: Echo: each person shares an assumption they checked. Then 'argue the other side' warm-up on a low-stakes topic.
flow.teach: Perspective-taking is constructing a model of someone else's view — and you build it by connecting to times you felt similar (Velcro). It doesn't mean you agree; it means you understand. That understanding is what makes solutions possible.
flow.practice: Conflict scenario (group disagreement over plans). Each person argues the OTHER side's view honestly, linking it to a personal memory.
flow.close: Each person finishes: 'Even though I disagree, I get why they ___.'

contentQuestions.text: Why can you understand someone without agreeing with them?
How does connecting to your own memory deepen perspective-taking?
When is taking another's view hardest?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: does perspective-taking require agreement?
Describe a time you took someone's perspective this week.
How did understanding their view change the outcome?

title: Say It So They Hear It
brainConcept: Messages land when framed around the listener's existing concerns (Velcro); blame triggers their defenses.
objective: I can advocate for what I need using an I-statement without escalating.

flow.connect: Echo: each person shares a perspective they took. Then a quick 'rewrite the blame' warm-up (turn 'you always' into an I-statement).
flow.teach: Blame triggers the other person's alarm and shuts down listening. I-statements ('I feel ___ when ___ because ___; could we ___?') frame the issue around shared concerns, keeping their thinking brain online. Tone and timing matter as much as words.
flow.practice: Real-ish scenario: a friend keeps borrowing money and not paying back. Draft and rehearse an I-statement; partner gives feedback on tone.
flow.close: Each person shares one I-statement they could use in a real situation this week.

contentQuestions.text: Why does blame make people stop listening?
How does framing around shared concerns help?
Why do tone and timing matter as much as the words?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what's the I-statement frame?
Did you use an I-statement this week? What happened?
What conversation are you avoiding that an I-statement could help?

title: Fix-It Talk
brainConcept: Rehearsing a repair script makes it retrievable under stress (Echo / retrieval practice).
objective: I can repair a conflict by owning my part and proposing a fair fix.

flow.connect: Echo: each person recalls the I-statement frame. Then a 'cool-down first' reset breath.
flow.teach: Repair beats winning. Steps: cool down, each shares their view without interrupting, own your part honestly, agree on a concrete fix. Owning your part lowers the other person's defenses fastest. Rehearsal makes the script available when you're upset.
flow.practice: Role-play: a rumor spread between friends. Pairs run all four steps, then swap roles. Debrief which step was hardest.
flow.close: Each person names the four steps and the one they need to practice.

contentQuestions.text: Why is owning your part so powerful in a repair?
What makes a fix feel fair to both sides?
Why rehearse the steps before you actually need them?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: list the four Fix-It steps.
Describe a conflict you repaired this week.
Which step is hardest for you, and why?

title: Stop & Plan
brainConcept: Peer presence raises risk-taking; a deliberate pause re-engages the prefrontal cortex.
objective: I can pause and run a decision process, even under peer pressure.

flow.connect: Echo: each person names a Fix-It step. Then a quick 'pause power' demo (resist an impulse for 10 seconds).
flow.teach: Research shows people your age take more risks when peers are watching — the reward system lights up. The counter-move is a pause that lets the prefrontal cortex run STOP: Stop, Think options + outcomes, weigh against values, Proceed. A pre-decided exit line ('I'm good, thanks') makes it easier.
flow.practice: Pressure scenario: friends want you to sneak out / vape / pile on someone online. Run STOP as a group; script a graceful exit line each.
flow.close: Each person shares their personal exit line.

contentQuestions.text: Why do people take more risks around peers?
How does a pause change the decision?
What makes an exit line easy to actually use?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what does STOP stand for?
When did you pause under pressure this week?
What's a situation you should pre-plan an exit line for?

title: Weigh It Out
brainConcept: Decisions improve when you simulate longer-term outcomes, not just immediate rewards.
objective: I can weigh short-term vs. long-term outcomes against my values.

flow.connect: Echo: each person shares their exit line. Then 'fast-forward' warm-up: predict where a choice leads in a week.
flow.teach: The reward system loves now; wise decisions also picture later. Weigh outcomes across time (now / this week / this year) and across people (you / others), then check against your values. This is mental time travel made deliberate.
flow.practice: Dilemma: copy an assignment due tomorrow vs. turn in your own incomplete work. Map short- and long-term outcomes; identify which values are in play.
flow.close: Each person finishes: 'The choice that matches my values is ___ because ___.'

contentQuestions.text: Why does the brain over-weight immediate rewards?
How does looking at long-term outcomes change the choice?
Which of your values matter most in this dilemma?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what two dimensions do we weigh outcomes across?
Describe a choice where you considered the long term this week.
When is it hardest to choose the long-term option?

title: Put It Together
brainConcept: Integrated skills form a schema that's faster and more reliable to deploy than isolated steps.
objective: I can sequence multiple Brain Lab tools to navigate a complex, realistic situation.

flow.connect: Rapid Echo: name as many tools as the group can in 45 seconds.
flow.teach: Mastery is having a connected web you can run automatically. Real situations stack: regulate first, read the other person, communicate, then decide. The order matters — you can't think clearly until you're regulated.
flow.practice: Layered scenario: you're publicly called out in a group chat. Group maps the full tool sequence and acts out the strongest response.
flow.close: Each person identifies their strongest tool and their weakest.

contentQuestions.text: Why must regulation usually come before communication?
Which tools fit the group-chat callout, and in what order?
Where do your tools tend to break down?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: name five Brain Lab tools.
Describe a time you ran a tool sequence this week.
Which tool will you strengthen next?

title: Rewire & Reflect
brainConcept: Spaced repetition and retrieval over time consolidate skills into durable, low-effort habits.
objective: I can set a concrete plan to keep practicing a skill so it becomes automatic.

flow.connect: Echo lap: circle the group naming each tool and its purpose until exhausted.
flow.teach: Six weeks of spaced practice + recall is exactly how the brain builds lasting pathways. The skills won't maintain themselves — habits need cues and repetition. Today you build a maintenance plan.
flow.practice: Each person writes a Rewire plan: target skill, trigger cue, when/where, and how they'll track it. Share with a partner for accountability.
flow.close: Each person names one concrete way they've changed since session one.

contentQuestions.text: Why does spaced practice create lasting change?
Which skill gave you the biggest payoff?
What cue will trigger your continued practice?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what's your Rewire plan?
How are you different from session one?
Who will hold you accountable?
